frameworks.name: Generate Flow, Monetize Flow, Add Friction
frameworks.definition: A three-stage acquisition sequencing model: start with the highest-volume (free or deeply discounted) front end to generate lead flow, monetize that flow, then progressively add friction to raise lead quality over time.

frameworks.name: Four Ways to Display Discounts
frameworks.definition: A framework for structuring discount offers across four display formats (dollar off, percentage off, buy-X-get-Y, limited-time unit price) so businesses can test which representation drives the highest response rate for a given price point.

frameworks.name: Five Friction Levers
frameworks.definition: Five controllable mechanisms for raising free-offer lead quality while reducing volume: increased qualifications, information requirements and question types, number of steps, forced consumption of sales material, and advertisement length.

frameworks.name: Two-Step Sale
frameworks.definition: A sales architecture that splits the close into two appointments: a low-cost or discounted first transaction that captures card-on-file and builds trust, followed by a second appointment where the core high-ticket offer is sold as an upsell.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that switching a front-end offer from a nominal price (e.g., $0.01) to free typically reduces lead cost by 5x or more, based on four independent split tests across ten representative gym markets each.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: gym/fitness market
local market with finite addressable eyeballs
front-end offer, not the core premium offer
claims.mechanism: A free offer generates a higher proportion of raised hands per ad impression because it removes the purchase friction entirely, reducing cost-per-lead proportionally.

numbers.axis: lead cost multiplier vs. non-free front end

bounded_by: Does not apply if the free offer itself has a hard per-unit cost that makes high volume unprofitable
Premium offers outperform on lead quality and per-sale profit even if lead cost is higher

claims.text: Alex Hormozi found, across four separate tests in gym markets, that the sales close rate on free-offer leads was statistically equivalent to the close rate on non-free (paid) offer leads when funnel design and sales environment were held constant.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: same funnel and sales environment for both cohorts
gym/fitness market
tested four separate times
claims.mechanism: Close rate is primarily a function of salesperson conviction and process quality, not whether the prospect paid an entry fee; the belief that paid leads close higher is a salesperson limiting belief rather than an empirical outcome.
bounded_by: Assumes sales environment and scripts are held constant; a weaker sales environment on the free side would produce a different result
Does not claim average ticket size is identical

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a high-ticket offer ($10,000 at $9,500 profit) presented to the same audience as a low-ticket offer ($100 at $50 profit) yields approximately 9.5x the profit from the same number of conversations, because roughly 5% of the low-ticket audience will close at the high-ticket price.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: audience already converted at low-ticket price ($100)
high-ticket offer presented to same 190 buyers
assuming ~5% close rate at $10,000 price point
claims.mechanism: A meaningful share of any buyer pool has budget for the premium tier; presenting it to the full pool captures that segment without changing who you talk to, compounding profit per conversation.

anchors.region: high-ticket offer ($10,000, $9,500 profit) at 5% close on same 190

bounded_by: Requires demonstrated value from a prior offer or selling process
5% close rate assumption may not hold in early-stage or low-trust markets

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that premium offers produce the highest-quality customers and reduce operational drag because only buyers with genuine budget and intent engage, eliminating low-intent prospects at the top of the funnel.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer priced at a tier that naturally filters uncommitted buyers
no discount or free-trial wrapper on the premium offer
claims.mechanism: Buyers who engage with a premium offer have already decided they are willing to pay; this self-selection removes freebie-seekers and price-shoppers before the sales conversation begins, reducing time spent qualifying.
bounded_by: Produces lowest raw lead volume of the three offer types
Requires strong copy and deep avatar knowledge to perform without a discount wrapper
Requires a proven sales process or cash reserves to absorb the higher cost-per-opportunity during learning

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that marginal discounts of 5-25% rarely drive meaningful behavioral change, whereas discounts of 50% or more reliably move a population that would not otherwise respond.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: discount must be on a well-understood service or product (e.g., gyms, dentists, chiropractors) so buyers have a reference price to compare against
claims.mechanism: Small discounts do not create a perceived value discrepancy large enough to override the status quo; large discounts create a discontinuous psychological shift by making inaction feel costly.

numbers.axis: discount percentage relative to behavioral change threshold

anchors.value: insufficient to drive action; primarily cuts margin

bounded_by: Discounting an unknown service is ineffective because buyers have no reference price to anchor against
Repeatedly discounting the core offer trains buyers to wait for discounts

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that discount front-end offers substantially eliminate appointment no-shows in service businesses because people consistently show up for things they have paid for, at roughly 85-90% or higher show rates, compared to lower rates for free-offer appointments.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service business where appointment time has a real cost (e.g., doctor, consultant)
small upfront payment collected at booking, card on file
claims.mechanism: Financial commitment creates sunk-cost motivation to attend; people who have paid something feel a loss if they skip, while free-offer prospects have no financial stake in showing up.
bounded_by: Requires actual card charge or pre-authorized fee, not merely a verbal commitment
Show rate improvement assumes fee is non-trivial relative to prospect's perceived value

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that the Two-Step Sale architecture makes each subsequent offer an upsell rather than a first sale, and that upselling an existing customer is substantially easier than converting a cold prospect into a first-time buyer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: first step involves a micro-purchase decision that captures card-on-file
upsell is presented after prospect has received tangible value from step one
higher prices especially benefit from multiple steps to allow trust to accumulate
claims.mechanism: The first micro-purchase shifts the prospect's identity from prospect to customer, lowering psychological barriers to a larger purchase; prior trust plus card on file eliminates the friction of a new payment decision.
bounded_by: Only effective if first-step experience delivers genuine value; a hollow first step reduces upsell conversion

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for discount offers to avoid training buyers to wait for deals, the discount must apply to a splinter (a component of the core offer), not the full core offer itself.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: business does not intend to use discount pricing as its permanent revenue model
discount is used as customer acquisition tool, not a permanent pricing strategy
claims.mechanism: When a discounted price repeatedly appears on the full core offer, buyers anchor to that price as normal and resist paying full price; discounting a component avoids anchoring the full-offer price to a discount level.
bounded_by: Exception: clothing retailers and similar businesses that deliberately inflate regular-season prices and live on discount periods; this is a conscious business model choice, not a failure mode

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that adding a free or heavily discounted front end to an existing premium offer increases lead volume and allows friction to be layered in progressively to maintain quality, rather than starting with friction and sacrificing early demand.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: entering a cold or new market
insufficient volume to optimize sales process on premium-only leads
cost of acquisition is higher than current business can bear
claims.mechanism: Free or discount wrappers lower the perceived risk of engaging for cold-audience prospects, converting a portion who would never respond to a bare premium offer, creating sufficient lead flow to iterate on sales process and generate proof.
bounded_by: Premium standalone offers remain superior once reputation, proof, and sales process are established
Wrapper does not change what is inside the offer; it only affects presentation and initial barrier to entry

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that researcher Dan Ariely demonstrated 9x more people take a free Hershey kiss than one priced at one penny, illustrating that the psychological gap between free and any price is discontinuously large.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: consumer product with immediate tangible gratification
low-stakes context (candy, not high-commitment service)
claims.mechanism: Zero price eliminates loss-aversion entirely; even a single penny activates the mental accounting of trade and triggers cost-benefit evaluation that free does not, reducing uptake by approximately 9x.
bounded_by: Effect size specific to low-stakes, immediately consumable products; high-ticket service purchases involve additional trust and delivery risk that the penny-gap dynamic does not capture alone

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that free offers are the correct diagnostic tool for new markets: if a free offer generates no response, the failure indicates one of three things - the wrong product/description, a credibility problem, or a targeting/fishing-pond mismatch.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: offer is positioned as genuinely free with no material hidden barrier
used as market validation, not primary revenue model
claims.mechanism: Because free removes price as a conversion barrier, any remaining non-conversion must trace to product-market fit (wrong offer), believability (promise too good to be true), or targeting (wrong audience pool); the three failure modes are mutually exclusive and actionable.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that structuring the initial free-offer visit as an admin-only process (paperwork, X-rays, financing pre-approval) rather than using the practitioner's time eliminates no-show cost while pre-qualifying all candidates before they see the professional.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service businesses where practitioner time is expensive (e.g., medical, dental, professional services)
initial appointment serves as qualification step, not delivery step
claims.mechanism: Separating admin pre-qualification from practitioner time means no-shows cost only admin overhead, not practitioner time; only pre-qualified, card-on-file prospects reach the sale conversation, raising conversion rate at the point of highest marginal cost.
bounded_by: Requires a step that can be fully delegated to non-specialist staff
Not applicable to businesses where first contact must involve the expert (e.g., highly technical initial assessments)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that all businesses capitalize on an information advantage: knowing more about a customer's problems than the customer does allows the business to anticipate future problems and profit through upsells.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: business has deeper domain expertise than the buyer
customer journey involves sequential problems (solving problem A exposes problem B)
claims.mechanism: Expert knowledge of the customer journey reveals downstream problems the buyer is unaware of; presenting those problems at the right moment and offering solutions converts them into upsells rather than surprises, increasing LTV without increasing acquisition cost.
bounded_by: Fails if the business exploits the information advantage deceptively; sustainable only when upsells genuinely solve real problems the customer will encounter

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that discount front-end offers work best as customer acquisition tools for well-understood services (gyms, dentists, chiropractors, hair salons) where prospects already have a reference price, and are ineffective for novel or unpriced services where no reference exists.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: service or product must be familiar to the prospect
prospect must have a reference price to perceive the discount as attractive
claims.mechanism: A discount is meaningful only relative to an expected price; if the prospect has no price expectation, a 50% discount of an unknown value is also unknown and creates no urgency or sense of gain.
bounded_by: Novel or complex offers benefit more from free offers than discount offers, since free removes the reference-price problem entirely

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that free offers with high-volume unqualified leads outperform lower-volume higher-quality discount offers on a pure dollar-and-cents basis, provided the free offer does not have a hard per-unit cost that makes volume unaffordable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: same ad spend for both cohorts ($1,000 in the worked example)
free offer has no material per-unit fulfillment cost
claims.mechanism: Higher raw volume of leads from free offers produces more total qualified leads in absolute terms even when the unqualified percentage is higher, as long as the cost of serving unqualified leads is low.
bounded_by: If serving each lead has a real cost (e.g., doctor's time), the math can flip in favor of the lower-volume higher-quality campaign

examples.text: In March 2016, Alex Hormozi spoke at a fitness industry conference at age 26. He presented a bare-bones black-and-white slide deck on the Free 6-Week Challenge he had used to pre-sell gyms to capacity before opening. After leaving the stage, he was mobbed for 60 minutes by hundreds of attendees asking if he had a course, coaching program, or consulting service. He declined every inquiry, saying he was only a gym owner. This was his first indication of external demand for what he knew, predating the launch of Gym Launch as a coaching business.

After the 2016 conference, Alex Hormozi created a fully free offer to launch Gym Launch: he would fly to a gym at his own expense, fund the marketing himself, work the leads, and close them. He kept all cash collected on the first sale and gave the gym the customers and a nutrition program at no charge. He ran this model across 33 gyms over 18 months, generating his first million dollars outside of his own gyms. The offer was easy to sell precisely because it was free, illustrating how a zero-price front end generates immediate flow even without an existing reputation.

Alex Hormozi describes a famous marketer who periodically ran a newspaper ad reading: 'For every $100 you give me I will give you $1,000 back, call 444-444-4444.' Despite being a mathematically superior offer, no one ever responded. The marketer ran it deliberately to illustrate that believability is a prerequisite for a free or too-good offer to convert; if an offer seems implausible, no amount of value will drive response.

Alex Hormozi illustrates the Two-Step Sale with a specific worked example: a business advertises a heavy metals test consultation for $19, collects the card over the phone, and sets an appointment. The prospect attends the $19 appointment, receives value, and is then booked into a follow-up appointment where a $2,100 ten-week detox plan is sold. The full funnel is: Ad → Opt-in → Phone call ($19 promo + appointment set) → Appointment → Value delivered → Follow-up appointment set → $2,100 program sold.

Alex Hormozi describes the smoothie-shop thought experiment: a hedge fund manager earning $50 million per year ($25,000/hour at 2,000 hours) bought a $50,000 burger with gold flakes and exotic caviar. At $25,000/hour, the burger cost the manager two hours of work. Hormozi, earning $6.75/hour at the time, also spent roughly two hours of work to buy a burger, fries, and soda for $13.50. The point: price is perceived relative to income and time value, not in absolute terms — illustrating why premium offers are rational to wealthy buyers.

practices.text: When entering a new market, start with a free or massively discounted offer to generate initial lead flow before adding friction or monetizing more aggressively.
Never structure a promotional offer so that free or discount changes the core product; treat it as wrapping paper around the same Grand Slam Offer.
If a free offer produces no response, diagnose which of the three failure modes applies (wrong product/description, credibility gap, or wrong targeting pool) before changing the offer price.
When using a discount front end, splinter the core offer into a small component piece to discount rather than discounting the full core offer, to avoid price-anchoring buyers at the discounted rate.

For service businesses with high-cost practitioner time, use the Proprietary 5-Minute Appointment Method: delegate the first visit to admin staff for paperwork and pre-qualification, and have the practitioner appear only briefly to confirm the follow-up appointment where the sale will be made.

To qualify free-offer leads without abandoning volume, add friction progressively: start with qualifications in ad copy, then application forms, then multi-step funnels, then forced video consumption before any CTA appears.
If new to a market or lacking strong conviction, use a free or deeply discounted offer to close initial sales, gather testimonials, and build proof before transitioning to premium pricing.

Match the entire acquisition strategy (price, prospect, process, promotion, product) to either a free-offer structure or a premium-offer structure; do not mix premium pricing with free-offer ad economics.
